data.title: Rare Stock Picks In March 2025 - From 15 Discerning Analysts
data.description:
Following the sell-off that began at the beginning of March (and accelerated in the first week of April), are there any compelling investment ideas out there that may have flown under your radar?

Below is a list of March Buy recommendations made by analysts who had no other bullish recommendations over the past 3 months.

For your directory assistance, we've classified the opportunities by sector.

Healthcare

EssilorLuxottica (OTCPK:ESLOF) - Investment analyst Harry Lynch says it is an industry leader with great economies of scale, great management and strong brand recognizability - the analyst plans to buy on any price weakness. - EssilorLuxottica: Quality Company, Here For The Long Haul

Financials

Sachem Capital (SACH) - Longtime analyst Miguel Marecos Duarte says the market's overly pessimistic view and management's improvements present a favorable investment opportunity while it has taken steps to stabilize its loan book, return to profitability, and reinstate its dividend. - The Market Is Too Pessimistic On Sachem Capital

KKR Real Estate Finance Trust (KREF) - Longtime analyst Allen Cooke says it is trading at a 25% discount to book value with a 9% yield, while improvements in commercial real estate are creating portfolio stabilization. - Improving Outlook At KKR Real Estate Finance Trust

Technology

Oracle (ORCL) - Longtime analyst High Watermark Investments says its strategic capex investments position it to capitalize on future growth, while the increased dividend and share buybacks indicate confidence in future cash flow improvements. - Oracle: Durable Growth Can Deliver Good Returns

Consumer Staples

Philip Morris International (PM) - Longtime analyst Logan Kane says higher sales and better margins paint a profitable picture, and it is expanding production to meet demand, expecting substantial sales growth this year. - Philip Morris International Is On Fire With Zyn: More Gains Ahead?

Consumer Discretionary

Sally Beauty (SBH) - Tarek Antaki, who has 20 years of experience working for two long/short hedge funds, says it is trading at a 13% FCF yield with potential to increase FCF by 30% within a few years, and it is nearly done with its multi-year deleveraging process, which will leave more capital to buy back stock and/or pay an attractive dividend. - Sally Beauty Holdings: Free Cash Flow Machine Meets Less Debt Paydown Requirements

Ulta Beauty (ULTA) - Longtime analyst AmericanStockPickers says it is trading at a steep discount to historical valuation metrics with resilient operating performance, consistent profitability, a clean balance sheet and shareholder-friendly management. - Ulta Beauty: Getting More Attractive With Each Visit, Just Like Its Customers

Carvana (CVNA) - Longtime analyst Himalayas Research says its strong market position and operational infrastructure present a compelling rebound opportunity at current valuation levels, while it recently reported strong earnings. - Carvana: A Comeback Possible With Tariff Easing

D.R. Horton (DHI) - SCIRE Investments, who has an opportunistic investment approach across the capital structure, says its current valuation and intermediate-to-long term growth prospects present an attractive entry point for long-term investors, as it has a history of market share growth, strong management, and prudent capital allocation. - D.R. Horton: A Coffee Can Compounder

Industrials

OPENLANE (KAR) - Longtime analyst Mike Arnold says it trades with an attractive 9-10x EBITDA multiple (a significant discount to peers despite strong growth) giving investors a near-term and long-term shot on goal to make solid returns while it operates in a fragmented market that is ripe for consolidation. - Will Openlane Appoint A CFO Or Sell Itself, That Is The Question

Jacobs Solutions (J) - Longtime analyst Matthew Levy says its diversified revenue streams, strategic focus, and attractive entry point make it a compelling investment opportunity, while it is focusing on margin expansion and divesting non-core segments for a leaner structure. - Get Into Jacobs Solutions Before It Solves All Of Its Problems

Fastenal (FAST) - Anton Davydkin, who focuses on large caps, says its diversified product portfolio, efficient operations, and high-quality earnings make it a top pick for long-term investors. - Fastenal: Promising Pick For Long-Term Investors Who Value Sustainability And Business Model Quality

Materials

POSCO (PKX) - Value investor Ryan Messick says it is undervalued, trading at less than half tangible book value, despite consistent profitability and a strong balance sheet, while it returns 50% to 60% of free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. - POSCO Holdings: A Value Stock With Consistent Profitability

Energy

Hallador Energy (HNRG) - Longtime analyst Jay Yoon says it appears to be nearing a transformative datacenter deal, which would contract most of its generation capacity for 10+ years, potentially driving EPS to $3.03 by FY 2027. - Hallador Energy: Recent Filing To MISO Indicates A Datacenter Deal Is Imminent

Vista Energy (VIST) - Longtime analyst Patrick Kroneman says despite risks, its valuation remains attractive - it has shown significant revenue and asset expansion, while its strategy focuses on reinvesting cash flow into growth, with plans to significantly increase well drilling and production in the Vaca Muerta formation. - Vista Energy: A Promising Play On The Vaca Muerta Formation

Inclusion Criteria: The above ideas were objectively compiled based on the set criteria. Exclusions include new Seeking Alpha analysts, broad market calls, crypto ideas, leveraged ETFs, and stocks that were repeat recommendations by the analyst.

Thanks for reading.

This account (Rare Stock Picks) will publish future iterations of this report in the upcoming months. If you missed our Rare Stock Picks from February, you can find it here.

data.title: The Manitowoc Company: Shares Have Gotten Dirt Cheap
data.description:
When it comes to investing, I have a pretty good track record. But nobody, and I do mean nobody, has a perfect record. Unfortunately, one company that I have been wrong about so far is The Manitowoc Company (MTW). This is a business that produces and sells equipment such as hydraulic cranes, lattice boom crawler cranes, boom trucks, tower cranes, and more. Back in February of last year, I believed that the enterprise was attractively priced. Shares were cheap and, while there were some weak spots, the overall trend for revenue leading up to that point had been encouraging. We were starting to see some meaningful declines, such as revenue later in that year and backlog both falling. But even factoring that weakness into the equation, the stock looked cheap enough to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

Since that time, the S&P 500 is down 0.4%. Over the same window of time, however, The Manitowoc Company has experienced a downside of 40.9%. This comes on the back of mixed financial results and concerns about the economy more broadly. While I do think that current conditions could pose a problem for the firm, the stock is truly too cheap to ignore. Because of broader economic concerns at the moment, I won't go so far as to upgrade the company from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy’. But if we do see this tariff problem that we are contending with disappear in short order, then an upgrade would certainly be justified.

A look at recent results

Fundamentally speaking, the picture has been rather mixed for The Manitowoc Company as of late. Take the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years as an example. Year over year, revenue contracted from $2.23 billion to $2.18 billion. This drop was driven by weakness across two of the three geographic regions of the company. In the Americas, revenue declined from $1.21 billion to $1.20 billion. This drop of 1.1% was because of an unfavorable change in product mix.

In its EURAF (Europe and Africa) segment, the company saw an even more drastic decline of 8% from $669.6 million to $616 million. A reduction in new crane shipments was the big driver here. However, management did not really provide much in the wave detail as to why this occurred. The only strength for the company came from its MEAP (Middle East and Asia Pacific) segment. Revenue for that part of the company actually jumped by 5% from $347 million to $364.4 million. This was because of higher non-new machine sales caused by an increase in demand for crane shipments. The firm also saw an $8.1 million rise in revenue associated with new machine sales thanks to a favorable change in product mix.

With revenue falling, would expect profitability to drop as well. However, the situation was more complicated than that. Net income actually rose from $39.2 million to $55.8 million. However, this requires some explanation. Part of this improvement came from a reduction in engineering, selling, and administrative costs from $328.3 million to $315.7 million. This stemmed from a $12.3 million reduction in legal costs associated with an EPA situation. Lower employee related costs of $3.1 million also helped. An even bigger contributor, however, was the company received a $44.1 million tax benefit for the year. That compared to the $5 million provision that it had to set aside the previous year. Even on an adjusted basis, there was a decline, with profits falling from $54.5 million to $14.7 million.

Other profitability metrics were, quite frankly, depressing. Operating cash flow as an example. This fell from $63 million to $49.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $110.6 million to $49.4 million. Obviously, we should also be paying attention to EBITDA. In 2023, this metric was $175.3 million. Fast-forward one year later, and it had fallen to $128.4 million.

In the final quarter of 2024, The Manitowoc Company began to show some more favorable results. In the chart above, you can see that revenue, profits, and three of the four cash flow metrics for the company improved. While this could be just a blip on the radar, it's more likely the start of consistent improvement for the company.

For 2025, management expects revenue to be between $2.175 billion and $2.275 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 3.3% above what the company generated in 2024. They also expect EBITDA to be between $120 million and $145 million. Adjusted earnings per share of between $0.15 and $0.85 should translate to adjusted net profits, at the midpoint, of $17.8 million. And finally, it is looking as though operating cash flow will be between $102 million and $132 million.

Using these estimates, as well as historical figures for the 2024 fiscal year, you can see how the stock is currently valued. On an absolute basis, shares strike me as incredibly cheap. This is especially the case if we assume that 2025 figures end up coming into play. Even relative to similar companies, shares are a bit on the cheap side. In the chart below, I stacked The Manitowoc Company up against five other similar enterprises. On a price to operating cash flow basis, you can see that only two of the five companies are cheaper than it is. This number drops to one of the five if we use the EV to EBITDA approach.

Long term, I believe that this is a very attractive candidate. But short term, it could be a very bumpy ride. Because of the recently announced reciprocal tariffs that have been applied by the Trump Administration, and the prospect of retaliatory tariffs, there could be some near term weakness. About 48.7% of the company's revenue comes from the US. However, the rest comes from overseas, with 27.5% of total sales attributable to Europe. Now, to be fair, as the image below illustrates, Europe has become less important to the company over the past few years. This has been offset by growth in the US and growth in other countries. But that growth in other countries could also come under pressure because of tariffs, since the recently announced ones apply to the globe.

In addition to a majority of revenue coming from overseas, it is worth noting that most of what the company produces very likely comes from outside of the US. And even what is produced in the US almost certainly relies on heavily imported parts. The reason why I say very little likely comes from the US is because, of the material manufacturing facilities that the company has in operation, only one of them, out of eight, is located here at home. They have one in Germany, one in Italy, two in France, one in Portugal, one in China, and another in India.

Another issue is that backlog and orders have been quite weak. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. By the end of the most recent quarter, backlog had fallen to $650.2 million. That's down from the $917.2 million reported one year earlier. The good news is that we have seen orders improve slightly, rising from $475.7 million to $515.6 million. But even with that increase on a year-over-year basis, orders are still too low to allow the backlog to grow, let alone stabilize. This tariff situation risks making that picture worse.

Takeaway

At this time, I believe that The Manitowoc Company is a really attractive prospect. I am worried about the situation involving tariffs. Only because of that have I decided not to upgrade the company. However, in the event that we do see some resolution on that front, this stock would be cheap enough to justify a very bullish outlook. For now, however, I am keeping it rated a ‘buy’.

Manager Commentary

“The fourth quarter of 2024 delivered mixed investment results across risk assets, with investor focus squarely centered on the U.S. elections and resulting policy as the prevailing driver of asset returns.” - Harbor Multi-Assets Solutions Team

Market in Review

The fourth quarter of 2024 delivered mixed investment results across risk assets, with investor focus squarely centered on the U.S. elections and resulting policy as the prevailing driver of asset returns. U.S. equities added to gains from previous quarters as the S&P 500 Index returned 2.4% during the fourth quarter, extending year-to-date gains to 25%. Small caps continued to lag their larger peers with the Russell 2000® Index returning 0.3% for the quarter and 11.5% year-to-date. U.S. equities were supported by expectations that Trump’s economic policies would increase economic growth, lower taxes, and reduce regulation. Within the U.S. market, momentum continued as growth equities continued to outperform value peers. The Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors also performed strongly. International equities lagged U.S. peers as the prospect of Trump’s proposed policy to increase tariffs on goods imported to the United States weighed on shares. Fixed-income markets experienced elevated volatility during the quarter, driven by increased geopolitical tensions, fluctuating inflation expectations, and changes in central bank policies. U.S. Treasuries sold off with concerns about an inflationary Trump policy agenda and sticky inflationary measures, leading markets to anticipate fewer rate cuts in 2025 and bond yields to rise in sympathy. The U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) cut rates for the third consecutive meeting, but Fed Chair Powell acknowledged there may be fewer-than-expected cuts ahead due to inflationary pressures. In Europe, central banks continued to cut rates, but fears of government instability in France and budgetary concerns in the United Kingdom dampened bond returns during the quarter.

Portfolio Performance

During the quarter, the Harbor Human Capital Factor US Small Cap ETF (“ETF”) returned –0.21% (NAV), underperforming the Human Capital Factor Small Cap Index (the “Index”), which returned –0.06%, and the Russell 2000® Index, which returned 0.33%. The ETF seeks to provide investment results that correspond, before fees and expenses, to the performance of the Index.

Contributors and Detractors

Top contributors include Brinker International and Dutch Bros, while Intellia Therapeutics and Surgery Partners detracted. Of the top 10 detractors during the quarter, seven are included in the Health Care sector.

Buys and Sells

The Index is developed by Irrational Capital and uses a rules-based methodology based on Irrational Capital's research in behavioral science and human capital. Considerations in the Index selection process include the translation of nonfinancial information into metrics that offer insight into financial performance, (i.e., quantifying the impact of corporate culture and employee motivation). Human capital is a broad, customizable, high-capacity, and multi-faceted category, and the ability to unlock its potential in a systematic and replicable manner has largely been untapped. Irrational Capital has developed this Index, which asserts that companies with the best human capital lead to long-term value creation and compelling equity returns. The Index is reconstituted annually by Irrational Capital and rebalanced quarterly to its rules-based modified market capitalization weight for each company. The index owns 183 positions.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

Sector overweights and underweights are not applicable. The Index is developed by Irrational Capital and uses a rules-based methodology based on Irrational Capital’s research in behavioral science and human capital. Considerations in the Index selection process includes the translation of nonfinancial information into metrics that offer insight into financial performance, i.e., quantifying the impact of corporate culture and employee motivation. Human capital is a broad, customizable, high capacity, and multi-faceted category, and the ability to unlock its potential in a systematic and replicable manner has largely been untapped. Irrational Capital has developed this Index, which asserts that companies with the best human capital led to long-term value creation and compelling equity returns. The Index is reconstituted annually by Irrational Capital and rebalanced quarterly to its rules-based modified market capitalization weight for each company.

Outlook

The Index is developed by Irrational Capital and uses a rules-based methodology based on Irrational Capital’s research in behavioral science and human capital. Considerations in the Index selection process includes the translation of nonfinancial information into metrics that offer insight into financial performance, i.e., quantifying the impact of corporate culture and employee motivation. Human capital is a broad, customizable, high capacity, and multi-faceted category, and the ability to unlock its potential in a systematic and replicable manner has largely been untapped. Irrational Capital has developed this Index, which asserts that companies with the best human capital led to long-term value creation and compelling equity returns. The Index is reconstituted annually by Irrational Capital and rebalanced quarterly to its rules-based modified market capitalization weight for each company.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time, the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Important Information

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3-Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Manager Commentary

"We are conscious of valuations becoming elevated and aware of risks arising from unexpected outcomes in global elections or geopolitics, but our base case is for continued economic growth with easing global financial conditions.” - Harbor Multi-Assets Solutions Team

Market in Review

The fourth quarter of 2024 delivered mixed investment results across risk assets, with investor focus squarely centered on the U.S. elections and resulting policy as the prevailing driver of asset returns. U.S. equities added to gains from previous quarters as the S&P 500 Index returned 2.4% during the fourth quarter, extending year-to-date gains to 25%. Similarly, the Russell 1000® Index returned 2.8%. Small caps continued to lag their larger peers with the Russell 2000® Index returning 0.3% for the quarter and 11.5% year-to-date. U.S. equities were supported by expectations that Trump’s economic policies would increase economic growth, lower taxes, and reduce regulation. Within the U.S. market, momentum continued as growth equities continued to outperform value peers. The Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors also performed strongly. International equities lagged U.S. peers as the prospect of Trump’s proposed policy to increase tariffs on goods imported to the United States weighed on shares. Fixed-income markets experienced elevated volatility during the quarter, driven by increased geopolitical tensions, fluctuating inflation expectations, and changes in central bank policies. U.S. Treasuries sold off with concerns about an inflationary Trump policy agenda and sticky inflationary measures, leading markets to anticipate fewer rate cuts in 2025 and bond yields to rise in sympathy. The U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) cut rates for the third consecutive meeting, but Fed Chair Powell acknowledged there may be fewer-than-expected cuts ahead due to inflationary pressures. In Europe, central banks continued to cut rates, but fears of government instability in France and budgetary concerns in the United Kingdom dampened bond returns during the quarter.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor AlphaEdge Large Cap Value ETF (“ETF”) returned –0.90% (NAV), underperforming the Harbor AlphaEdge Large Cap Value Index, which returned –0.82%, and outperforming its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Value Index (“Index”), which returned –1.98%.

Overall, the ETF outperformed the benchmark due to strong sector allocation. The Health Care and Financials sectors were positive contributors to relative performance versus the benchmark. Our underweight allocation to Health Care was a contributing factor, while the Health Care sector in the benchmark underperformed due to concerns about the administration’s potential policy changes having a negative impact on profitability. Financials gained due to investor perception that the new administration’s deregulation stance would benefit earnings within the sector.

The ETF’s stock selection within the Consumer Discretionary and Energy sectors detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in refiners within the Energy sector and homebuilders within Consumer Discretionary were also drags on relative performance.

Outlook

Our business cycle regime indicator continues to signal late-cycle economic conditions with market sentiment exhibiting risk-seeking behavior. Economic growth remains resilient in the U.S., as third quarter Gross Domestic Product growth came in at a healthy 3.1% on the back of strength in consumer spending. In the U.S., the incoming administration has signaled its desire to implement pro-growth policies such as tax relief and deregulation. Outside of the U.S., the incoming administration’s focus on tariffs will keep expectations for global growth under pressure due to uncertainty on trade policy. Central banks around the world continued to cut interest rates in response to increased confidence from authorities that we are well past peak inflationary pressures. The Fed cut rates by 25 basis points in consecutive meetings during the quarter. This was viewed as a hawkish cut, which tempered market expectations for the potential number of additional cuts in 2025. As our base case, we expect risk assets to experience continued positive returns in the coming quarters, with economic growth remaining resilient, but we are focusing on how new policy measures may affect the inflationary outlook and central bank policy going forward. We are conscious of valuations becoming elevated and aware of risks arising from unexpected outcomes in global elections or geopolitics, but our base case is for continued economic growth with easing global financial conditions.
