Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3-Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Retiring On Passive Income
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Today, I want to talk about "rules". We at High Dividend Opportunities have developed a strategy that we call it, "the Income Method". We created various rules that we follow when managing our investment accounts. Let me be clear: These are purely voluntary guidelines that we place on ourselves to ensure that we are not taking unnecessary risks.

We often receive questions looking for a verdict on how our rules should be applied. For example, a common question is about our rule to limit position sizes to 3-4% for each holding. What if the same company has two different securities, like a bond and a common stock? Should we limit ourselves to 3% each for 6% combined? Or should we split our 3% investment between the two?

These aren't laws that are meant to be rigidly applied, and they aren't engraved on stone tablets. These are not based on some fundamental Truth. These are guidelines that we voluntarily follow because we believe they help make our portfolio stronger.

Sometimes, rules can be broken. Growing up, mom had a rule: No eating dinner in front of the TV. We ate as a family at the dinner table with no TV on. A good rule that led to healthy family conversation and bonding. But sometimes, mom would pick up a pizza and put a movie on the TV, and we would all sit on the floor, eating our dinner while watching it. Did that occasional rule-breaking harm anything? No.

If you understand why a rule exists, you can follow the spirit of the rule even if you occasionally violate the letter of the rule. Exercising a little bit of discretion and common sense is an important part of managing your portfolio.

We have the Rule of 42, the Rule of 25, we limit individual position sizes, and we have guidelines for our decision-making process.

Why We Have Rules

We can do anything we want. When we go to buy stocks, our broker isn't going to stop us from dumping 100% of our net worth and whatever amount of debt we can borrow into whatever meme stock the internet forums say we should HOLD to the moon with diamond hands.

We create rules for ourselves, and we do it because we aren't 20-somethings who feel invincible. We have learned, probably through making several big mistakes, that we are fallible. If you managed to be one of those smart people who learned solely through the mistakes of others, congratulations! Most of us learned that we aren't perfect through the School of Hard Knocks.

We all make mistakes, and when making investments with imperfect information about the future, we aren't going to be perfect. We are going to have some great investments, a lot of mediocre investments, a few bad investments, and a few really terrible investments—that's investing.

The problem is that you don't know what you are wrong about until the future proves to you that you were wrong. By that time, it's entirely too late.

Sometimes, we get things wrong at HDO. Our official track record is that we have exited 362 positions, and 80% of the time, we have exited with a profit. That means that 20% of the time, we realized a loss. We will always try to do our best, but the reality of the world is that we're never going to be perfect. We are always going to have some percentage of investments that result in a loss.

As we go into retirement, it is important that we recognize that we aren't going to be perfect. We are going to buy into investment ideas that don't work out, either through our own mistakes during due diligence or through a series of unfortunate events.

By creating guidelines, we can limit the impact our mistakes have on our overall portfolio, our income, and our retirement. It's ok to make mistakes, but it is not ok to have a retirement plan that allows room for mistakes.

The Rule Of 42

The "Rule of 42" is our target to diversify our portfolio among a minimum of 42 investments. Note that this is a minimum. The HDO Model Portfolio holds more than 120+ investments comprising common equities, funds, preferred equities, and bonds. I own them all, plus some additional holdings that don't quite fit into the Model Portfolio's specific goals.

In short, if you are following HDO, you should have no problem exceeding the target of 42 holdings, even if you find yourself disagreeing on the attractiveness of a particular investment or sector that we invest in. I believe that owning the entire HDO Model Portfolio is a good goal, and that's what I do myself.

Why is 42 the minimum? Well, because around 40 stocks, the benefit of diversifying further starts to fade. Source

It is easy to see how there is a huge diversification benefit for adding one stock to a portfolio that only has 1–2 stocks. But if your portfolio had 100 stocks, would adding one more make a material difference to your diversification? No.

The larger the portfolio, the less diversification benefits you get from adding one more investment. Around 40 stocks are where you have diversified almost all of the diversifiable risk.

We set the "Rule of 42" at 42, as a reminder not to take ourselves too seriously. 42 is the answer to "the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything". If you are at 38 tickers, you don't have to rush out and randomly buy four more. It's a target that you want to get to as you identify good investment opportunities. In time, it is a target you will likely exceed by a substantial amount.

We also want to apply some common sense to this target. If we buy 42 different oil investments, we aren't diversified. If we buy 42 REITs, we aren't diversified. As we add or remove holdings from the HDO Model portfolio, we are aware of whether we have similar holdings. We will frequently have 1-2 other stocks that are directly related because we do want to diversify within sectors, but we limit ourselves to having 2–3 holdings that are direct competitors. If you are assembling 42 holdings on your own, you'll want to take that step of asking how similar they are to each other.

Always keep the spirit of the rule in mind. The goal of Rule of 42 is to diversify our portfolios.

Allocation Limits

At HDO, we typically suggest limiting allocation sizes to 2% for single company tickers and 3-4% for funds. The reason for these limits is similar to the Rule of 42. If we have 42 holdings, our average position size will be 2.38%.

But sometimes, you might have a holding that outperformed recently, and now it is a larger allocation. Or maybe you just got a bit enthusiastic and bought more. If you have the common and the preferred for the same company, should you combine the allocations?

Let's go back to the core of why we have a limit.

Sometimes, we are going to be wrong. Sometimes, investments will cut their dividends, change their strategies, prices will crash, and sometimes companies go bankrupt.

When limiting our allocation, our goal is to limit the impact that any one bad investment will have on our retirement. We want to allow for catastrophe, whether it is something we should have predicted or something that completely blind sides us. Our allocations should be at a size where the following two things are true:

  1. If the investment is a complete disaster that results in a complete loss, the size is small enough that it will not materially impact our ability to continue funding our retirement.
  2. When bad news comes, the investment will be a small enough portion of our portfolio that panic will not have a significant influence on our decision-making.

The first one is easy. If we assume a 100% loss, which even in extreme cases is larger than what typically happens, losing 2-3% of your portfolio is a moderately bad week in the market. It isn't necessarily going to guarantee that you have a bad year. The stock market makes much larger gyrations all the time.

The second one is more individualized. Some investors can face huge losses in their portfolio and maintain the ability to keep a clear head and make rational decisions. Many other investors can't. I believe that with position sizes of 2-3%, most investors are able to look at bad news for a position without panic. They are comfortable with making the decision to hold or take the loss based on their outlook of what might happen in the future, rather than out of fear for their financial situation. If you find yourself getting overly stressed when a particular investment swings 10-20%, then perhaps your position sizes should be smaller. Only you can say where the line is that is comfortable for you.

When it comes to a company where you might hold bonds, preferred, and/or common equity, I suggest going through the philosophical exercise of the "FBI Gut check". Pretend you wake up tomorrow morning and the FBI is raiding the offices of the company, and it is going to be the next Enron, which means a big fat zero from the entire capital stack. Fortunately, those events are very rare, but it does happen. Fraud does occur. So, whenever you are asking, "is it ok to own 5%, 6%, or 10% in this one company?" Do the FBI gut check and think of how you would feel.

If you are concerned about the security of your retirement, then your allocation is too high. If you could brush it off as bad luck and the bruising is mostly your ego, but you wouldn't be concerned about your ability to sustain your retirement, then your allocation is fine.

That line isn't the same for everyone.

The Rule of 25

The "Rule of 25" is the idea that you should reinvest 25% of your dividend income. We implement this rule for a variety of reasons.

First, we understand that we aren't perfect, and we will sometimes invest in companies that cut their dividends. By reinvesting at least 25% of our income, we ensure that we have a cushion so that dividend cuts can be absorbed without putting our daily budget at risk. A 25% cushion is likely larger than you will ever need, but it is better to be cautious. You don't want to be in a situation where your portfolio isn't producing enough to meet your basic essentials. By planning to reinvest 25%, you will have excess income to absorb cuts even through a period like the GFC or COVID, where dividend cuts were more frequent than usual.

Second, reinvesting dividends provides us with a reliable method of driving income growth. While many companies might raise their dividends, there is no guarantee that they will. When we take a portion of our dividends and reinvest them, our income grows even if the company never hikes its dividend. While we love it when the companies we invest in raise their dividends, having a built-in source of income growth through reinvestment is a powerful base that helps us with our goal of growing our income every single year—even in years when the economy is poor.

The more you reinvest, the faster your income will grow. So, investors should weigh their needs for cash today, with their desire to see greater growth in the future.

Conclusion

We think that the rules that we have in our Income Method are a good set of guidelines to help you ensure that your portfolio is protected, and that you have a retirement plan that can survive contact with reality. Our Income Method is an investment strategy that doesn't rely on us being perfect. It allows for mistakes, and the guidelines help us ensure that those mistakes won't threaten our overall retirement plan. If we happen to be perfect. . . Well, that isn't going to happen.

Do you have to strictly follow our guidelines to be successful? No. Maybe you own 35 stocks, or maybe you want to set 100 as your minimum. If you only need 50% of the cash flow from your portfolio to meet your retirement goals, maybe you are comfortable bumping up your allocation sizes and making bigger bets because it isn't a problem if one doesn't work out.

We present our strategy with rules that make sense for our average member. One size doesn't fit all, so feel free to customize your rules to fit your particular needs. What is most important is to understand why the rules are in place and the goals of those rules. It is important to construct your retirement plan with the understanding that you aren't perfect, you will make bad investments, and sometimes, the investment you thought was a "can't lose" idea loses.

The Income Method is a strategy that builds in the assumption that we will make mistakes. It helps us ensure that our mistakes don't put our entire retirement at risk.

data.title: Meta: Time To Buy The Fear [Upgrade]
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous cautious thesis about Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) aged well, as the stock has dipped by 11% since early October. The stock is currently extremely attractively valued, especially considering its stellar revenue growth and operating leverage. The pace of innovation and commitment to become the backbone of the AI era are impressive. All in all, I upgrade META back to "Strong Buy".

Recent developments

The company released its latest quarterly earnings on January 29, which were accompanied by a confident dual beat against consensus estimates. Meta delivered a strong 20.6% YoY revenue growth in Q4, which was an acceleration compared to the previous quarter's revenue growth.

The adjusted EPS growth was even more impressive, as the metric increased from $5.33 to $8.02 on a YoY basis. It was high-quality EPS growth as it was fueled by the operating margin expansion from 43.6% to 45.3%. Meanwhile, the outstanding share count was stable over the last several quarters.

With a robust operating leverage, Meta's free cash flow generating capacity remains strong. As a result, the company boasts a fortress balance sheet with a massive $28 billion net cash position. Such a strong position means that Meta is not only ready to weather any storm but also exercises vast financial flexibility to invest in innovation and business expansion.

The company remains quite aggressive in its AI endeavors as it continues ramping up its data center investments. Meta plans to build another large data center in Wisconsin, worth $1 billion. This is not just a separate data center, but a part of the company's ambitious plans in driving the global AI revolution. Overall, to ensure that it possesses sufficient capacity to handle increasing data demands and complex computations, Meta plans to pour $65 billion in its AI infrastructure during 2025.

The company also works hard on making its Llama LLM offering among the most advanced. Meta recently started releasing its updated Llama 4 family of models, which is a very important milestone in building the company's moat in the AI era. New features include significant improvements in natural language processing, which enable more sophisticated and context-aware interactions across Meta's products. In addition, Llama celebrated 1 billion downloads in March 2025.

According to the official release from Meta, the Llama 4 family looks very good compared to major LLM offerings. For example, Llama 4 Scout outperforms models like Gemma 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite, and Mistral 3.1 across several parameters. The biggest advantage, in my opinion, is that it offers an industry-leading 10 million token context windows. In addition, there is Llama 4 Maverick, which surpasses GPT-4o and Gemini 2.0 Flash on various benchmarks including coding, reasoning, multilingual, long-context, and image benchmarks. The company also says that Llama 4 Maverick's performance is comparable to DeepSeek v3.1 (DEEPSEEK).

Among recent promising developments, there is also a collaboration with Ultimate Fighting Championship [UFC]. The partnership aims to integrate Meta AI, Meta Glasses, and Meta Quest, into UFC's content delivery. I think that it is a quite promising endeavor since UFC has been quite rapidly gaining popularity over the last few years, especially with Generation Z. Therefore, this step increases Meta's chances to successfully expand into new user demographics.

From the secular trends' perspective, the picture remains bright for Meta. The global digital advertising market continues demonstrating robust growth, which is expected to sustain for longer. According to the forecast from Grand View Research, the global digital advertising market is expected to deliver a 15.4% market CAGR over the next five years.

Stock seasonality trends also look quite positive as the May-August period historically has been extremely successful with 70%-80% win rates for each month. In addition, there were several bullish rating reiterations from Jefferies, Cantor Fitzgerald, Citizens Capital Markets, and KeyBanc over the last couple of weeks.

Valuation update

Despite having a low "F" valuation grade from Seeking Alpha Quant, I think that Meta's valuation ratios look great. This is because most of its current multiples are lower than historical averages. I am not comparing to the sector median because Meta's multi-billion social media audience together with stellar profitability make it an absolutely unique company.

Looking only at valuation ratios might be too risky because multiples are mostly focused on either past performance or only short-term future. Therefore, it is better to also simulate a discounted cash flow [DCF] model. The discount rate for my DCF is 9.36%, please see calculations below.

As a conservative investor, I prefer to use the FCF margin that excludes the stock-based compensation [SBC]. In Meta's case, its current ex-SBC FCF margin is 11.8%. This was used as my base-year assumption. Due to Meta's historically strong operating leverage, I incorporated a quite aggressive 200-bps yearly expansion of the metric.

Revenue assumptions are based on consensus projections as their trajectory looks sound and conservative with a steady growth rate deceleration. My base-case scenario's perpetual growth rate is also quite conservative at 3%. The total fair value estimate in this case is $1.63 trillion, indicating a 27% upside potential.

I think that assuming a 4% perpetual growth rate for a company like Meta is also reasonable, which was my second DCF scenario for META. With a more aggressive perpetual growth rate, the business's fair value is closer to $1.9 trillion.

Risks update

President Trump's tariffs shocked the entire stock market, as the popular Fear & Greed Index is currently close to zero. META has lost around 12% over the last five days, and it is very uncertain whether we will see a sharp rebound, or the panic will take over the stock market for longer. My approach is simple, I dollar-average high-quality stocks when valuations become more attractive and willing to hold them for years. Trying to capitalize on this short-term extreme volatility might be too risky.

The regulatory environment around digital advertising and social media is very dynamic across the world. For example, the UK's Online Safety Act mandates stringent content moderation, and non-compliance might result in substantial fines and penalties.

Mark Zuckerberg is certainly the major star in the company's leadership team, meaning that we do not know very much about other key executives. Therefore, the expected departure of the company's head of AI research adds some uncertainty around Meta's future plans and innovation pace in AI.

Bottom line

To conclude, META is currently very attractively valued and certainly deserves the rating to be upgraded back to "Strong Buy". The company's fundamentals keep improving, and there is great potential to become the backbone of the AI era.

data.title: Taking A Small Position In Stanley Black & Decker (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It’s been about 3 ½ months since I announced to an indifferent world that I’m continuing to avoid Stanley Black & Decker Inc. (SWK) in an article with the stupendously creative title “Continue to Avoid Stanley Black & Decker.” Since then, the shares have returned a loss of about 31.25% against a loss of about 15.5% for the S&P 500. This is just the sort of result that my very fragile ego needs, and I’m going to milk every opportunity to revel in self-congratulation. I’m sure you can’t wait to read how in love with my own analysis I am. Unfortunately, though, there’s more to this article than self-love. When I recommended avoiding the stock, it was trading at $80.50. Today, it’s changing hands for $57. For that reason, it’s a much less risky investment today than it was when I last wrote about it a few months back. I therefore need to determine whether or not it represents good value. I’ll make that determination by looking at the latest financial results, and by comparing the stock to the risk-free Treasury Note.

It’s time again for my thesis statement. This is where I offer readers the gist of my thinking so they can get in, understand my thoughts, and get out before they’re exposed to too much of my self-congratulation and proper spelling. You’re welcome. Anyway, the last time I looked at this stock, the dividend yield was significantly below the yield on the Treasury Note. Today, though, the relationship has changed, and the dividend is now significantly higher than the yield on the 10-Year Note. While the problems I outlined about dividend sustainability that I wrote about previously persist, I think the probability of a cut is very low. I’m going to take advantage of the current market weakness by buying a small position in this business. We’re frequently told to “buy low”, and I feel the need to remind readers that in order to do that, you need to “pull the trigger” when it’s scary to do so.

Financial Snapshot

If you had only 2 years of history with this company, you’d be forgiven for being impressed by Stanley’s financial performance. Although sales were down about $415 million, or 2.6%, in 2024 compared to 2023, net income actually swung from a loss to a positive $286 million. Most of this improvement relates to a $997 million, or 8.4%, reduction in cost of sales. So, management managed to handle the drop in business quite well, and returned the company to profitability.

The problem emerges when you compare the latest financial results to previous years. For example, revenue in 2024 was about the same as it was in 2021, but net earnings are about $1.4 billion lower. That’s a “spicy meatball”, as the young people say.

Still, profitability is profitability, and if I determine that the dividend is sustainable, and if the stock represents good value, I’ll buy.

Dividend Sustainability

In my view, the sustainability of the dividend is of critical importance here. The reason is simple enough. If the dividend isn’t sustainable, it’s likely that the price will drop precipitously, as dividend investors become uncertain.

Although I love accrual accounting as much as it’s possible to love accrual accounting, I am of the view that when it comes to dividends and their sustainability, cash is king. So, I’m going to review Stanley’s likely sources, and uses of cash. I’m in the mood to start by looking at sources, so I’m going to start looking at sources of cash. As of the latest 10-K, the company has about $290.5 million in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet. Additionally, if we strip out the sales of businesses and sales of assets over the past four years, they’ve generated an average of $366.3 million in cash from operations. That written, over the same time period, they’ve generated about $4.877 billion from the sale of businesses, and about $71.6 million from the sale of other assets.

At the same time, the company has invested an average of about $455 million in the business annually.

In terms of obligations, I’ve plucked the following from page 42 of the latest 10-K for your enjoyment and edification.

It seems that the company is “on the hook”, as the cool kids say, for $1.855 billion this year, and an average of $959 million for each of the next two years.

Taking all of the above into account leads me to what I consider to be an awkward conclusion. If the company can continue to generate massive levels of cash from selling businesses, then the dividend is quite safe. If, as is more likely, however, they inevitably run out of businesses to sell, the dividend isn’t well covered in my view. Generating an average of $366 million, while needing to invest $455 million doesn’t fill me with optimism.

That written, I don’t think the company will do all that it can to cut or not continue to increase the dividend. This is, after all, a “dividend aristocrat”, and if we use the behaviour of real world human aristocrats as a guide, they’ll be willing to take on ever greater levels of debt to keep up appearances. So, I think cultural factors, more than economic ones, will continue to protect this dividend.

My regular readers know that when I wrote “good value” above, I meant “good value relative to the risk-free alternative.” In order for me to be convinced to buy these shares, I’d need them to offer a superior return when compared to the 10-Year Treasury Note. That’s what the next section of this article will focus on.

As usual, when I compare a stock to the Treasury Note, I review the rate at which the stock dividend needs to grow in order for the cash flows from each investment to be equal. I then work out whether or not this growth is likely given its financial history.

As of this writing, the yield on the 10-Year Treasury Note is about 4.4%, and the dividend yield is a very healthy 5.94%.

To refresh your understanding if your math’s a little weak, 5.94% is actually higher than 4.4%, so it’s possible for this dividend to actually drop and for the investors to still earn the same return as Treasury Note holders. If you were wondering at what rate the dividend can be cut for the cash flows to be equal, you’re in luck, because I’ve worked it out and have included my findings below. I hope this satisfies your boundless curiosity. Because the premium is so high, the dividend can fall at a CAGR of about 6.8% for the next decade for the cash flows to be equal. That’s a fairly decent margin of safety in my view.

How much extra income will a stock investor earn over the Treasury Note if the dividend grows at the same rate over the next decade as it has over this past one? For information’s sake, it’s grown at a CAGR of about 4% over the past decade, and I present the answer to that burning question in the table below. It seems that the stock investor will pick up an extra 62% over the Treasury Note holder. That makes Stanley a relatively rare stock in that it offers a premium to the risk-free alternative, and not a discount to it.

Since Stanley currently offers a decent risk premium, I’m willing to take a small position in this company. The shares may drop further from current levels, and if that happens I’ll buy more. The valuation seems reasonable, the company is profitable enough, and I highly doubt the dividend will be cut anytime soon. It would have to be cut materially to erode the risk premium, so I feel fairly confident in my bullish position here.

I’m going to finish this article with a small sermon, and I’m sure you can’t wait to be lectured at by some stranger on the internet. For months now I’ve been comparing every stock, usually unfavourably, to the Treasury Note. Many readers have been puzzled by this, and have objected that it’s not “fair” to compare the stock to a government bond. It would have been better, apparently, to do peer comparisons. In my view, that might lead me to discover which stock in a given industry is the “cleanest dirty shirt”, but would do nothing to help with risk-adjusted returns. I compare this and every other stock to the risk-free rate in order to benchmark it. If I cannot earn more from a risky stock than from the risk-free Treasury Note, why would I buy the stock? I’m earning less from it, while taking on more risk?! That makes zero sense, and this framework is what informs my analyses since the yield on the Treasury Note spiked.

data.title: Digital Realty: Great Growth And Income All In One
data.description:
Market downturns can be great times to add to your favorite names. While some would say to wait for a bottom, I have yet to see anyone who can consistently call a market bottom every time. That’s why it pays to layer in capital instead of going in all at once. This brings me to Digital Realty Trust (DLR), which at the current price of $142 trades well off its 52-week high of $198 reached in December.

I last covered DLR back in October 2023, highlighting its underlying strengths and growing demand for data centers due to cloud migration and AI. It appears that my thesis has worked out well, with the stock giving investors a 32% total return since my last piece despite the recent downturn in price. This far outpaced the 27.7% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. Who said real estate is boring compared to tech stocks?

In this article, I revisit DLR including its outlook and valuation and discuss why layering in at the current price makes sense for long-term income and growth, so let’s get started!

Why DLR?

Digital Realty Trust is a leading REIT that provides data center, colocation and interconnection solutions. Its 5,000+ customers include enterprise companies, cloud providers and hyperscalers. DLR’s 300+ data centers serve its clients in over 50+ metro areas. As shown below, DLR’s assets are diversified by geography on 6 continents with 2,700 MW of in place IT capacity and another 3,500+ MW of buildable IT capacity.

Meanwhile, DLR achieved robust results last year with full-year bookings exceeding $1 billion, which set a company record. This was driven by significant hyperscale deals and enterprise-focused deployments. Notably, DLR achieved $100 million in signed new leases and added a record number of new logos. Of that, a record $76 million was in the highly profitable 0-1 MW interconnection segment, representing 16% growth sequentially. Importantly, renewals approached a record $1 billion last year, with a strong cash rent spread of 9%.

At the same time, DLR carries a BBB credit rating with a stable outlook from S&P. It significantly improved its balance sheet leverage, with net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA declining significantly from 6.2x to 4.8x over the past 12 reported months. DLR also carries a strong $6 billion in total liquidity, including $3 billion in new capital raised last year through equity and debt offerings. Over the past 5 years, DLR has grown its revenue from $3.4 billion to $5.6 billion while keeping its debt-to-equity ratio in check, with it sitting at around the same level as where it was in 2021 at 0.83x.

Management is guiding for full-year core FFO per share growth of 5.7% to $7.10 at the midpoint of the range and anticipates normalized revenue and EBITDA growth of 10% this year. This is supported in large part by expected cash increases of 4% to 6% on renewals this year. DLR also maintains industry-leading sustainability practices with over 150 data centers powered by 100% renewable energy and is rolling out new products to enhance densities at its locations, as noted during the last earnings call:

We've rolled out new products and services such as high-density Colo 2.0, a cooling solution to support densities of up to 150 kilowatts per rack, the expansion of ServiceFabric to 38 metros around the world, and private AI Exchange, an open platform available through ServiceFabric, which enables enterprises to seamlessly integrate their data with AI capabilities and other technology solutions. By combining these leading-edge solutions with our global full-spectrum strategy of connective campuses that offer Colo, scale, and hyperscale capacity, customers can count on Digital Realty to meet all of their data center needs.

Over the long-term, DLR maintains a strong growth runway due to AI-led capacity blocks that support growth in cloud and digital transformation, while data center supply remains tight. According to McKinsey, global demand for data center capacity is expected to triple by the end of this decade. As shown below, the midrange scenario calls for 22% CAGR in demand for data center capacity, by measure of gigawatts.

Importantly for income investors, DLR yields a respectable 3.3% and the dividend is well-covered by a 69% payout ratio. While DLR’s 5-year dividend CAGR is just 2.3%, that’s due to DLR using retained capital to fund robust growth, much like how a tech company in a fast-growing industry operates.

Lastly, I find value in DLR at the current price of $145.77 with a forward P/FFO of 20.6. While this sits above its normal P/FFO of 17.5, I believe the premium is justified by the aforementioned robust growth trends driven by AI. Plus, given supply constraints, I believe DLR can sustain annual FFO/share in the high single-digit level over the medium term. This is supported by analyst estimates of 9.5% to 14.9% annually over the next 3 years. This, combined with a 3.3% dividend yield, could result in potential market-beating total returns from here.

Risks to Digital Realty income power availability in its areas of operations, since data centers require massive amounts of electricity. As such, surging power prices could result in lower margins for DLR should it be unable to pass along costs to its customers. DLR also has customers concentration in AWS (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG). If these players slow expansion or build their own data centers, then DLR could see growth pressures. Lastly, competition from private equity in building new data centers could result in higher supply and lower pricing power by DLR.

Investor Takeaway

Digital Realty Trust stands out as a strong long-term play in the data center space, with it benefiting from robust demand trends driven by AI, cloud adoption, and digital transformation. Despite recent market weakness, DLR has delivered solid returns, backed by strong leasing activity, healthy balance sheet improvements, and a clear growth path.

With a well-covered 3.3% dividend, expanding cash flows, and forward-looking innovation like high-density cooling and AI integration, DLR offers an attractive blend of income and growth. Given the strong catalysts around the company, I believe DLR is an appealing tech-REIT stock with a decent yield to boot for income and growth investors.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: TSMC Revenue Surges On Pre-Tariff Rush
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

TSMC Q1 sales top estimates on surging AI demand ahead of U.S. tariffs. (00:22) Amazon (AMZN) pulls the plug on China orders, leaving vendors in the lurch - Bloomberg. (02:03) U.S. Postal Service aims to raise stamp prices to 78 cents. (02:56)

This is an abridged transcript.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) reported a nearly 42% surge in Q1 revenue, fueled by strong demand for AI servers and smartphones ahead of looming U.S. tariffs.

First-quarter revenue totaled NT$839.25 billion ($25.6 billion), up 41.6% compared to the same period in 2024, marking the company’s fastest pace of growth since 2022. Analysts on average expected around NT$830.5 billion, as per Bloomberg.

March revenue also rose 46.5% Y/Y to approximately NT$285.96 billion as electronics makers had built up inventories in U.S. warehouses, preparing for possible trade disruptions.

In March, TSMC CEO C.C. Wei and President Donald Trump jointly unveiled a $100 billion expansion in U.S. chipmaking investments, supporting the White House’s push to restore manufacturing. Trump attributed the increased commitment to the impact of higher tariffs.

Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM), who customers include Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), and AMD (AMD), will report its full Q1 earnings on April 17. Investors will be watching for a possible downward revision to the full-year sales target and capital expenditure plans, as the outlook for global chip demand weakens amid uncertainty following new U.S. tariffs, Bloomberg Intelligence said.

The company expects net revenue in the range of US$25B-US$25.8B (consensus estimate: $25.52B) for 1Q25, with a gross margin of 57%-59%.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has cancelled orders that originate from China and Southeast Asian countries, leaving certain vendors on the hook with unsold inventory.

According to a document viewed by Bloomberg, Amazon appears to be reducing its exposure to U.S. tariffs on imported merchandise from China-based suppliers who provide a “significant portion” of Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) components and finished goods.

According to Bloomberg, a consultant to Amazon (AMZN) vendors claims the company has cancelled several “direct import orders” of products made in China without warning. Because Amazon (AMZN) acts as the “importer of record” on these orders, it is responsible for paying import tariffs once the merchandise arrives in the U.S. However, if the order is canceled once shipping is initiated, the tariff burden lies with the vendor once the merchandise reaches the U.S.

The United States Postal Service announced proposed price changes for its mailing services, set to take effect on July 13, pending approval from the Postal Regulatory Commission.

Among the most notable changes is a 5-cent increase in the price of a First-Class Mail Forever stamp, raising the cost from 73 cents to 78 cents.

The adjustments, approved by the USPS Board of Governors, represent an average price increase of approximately 7.4% across mailing services.

The agency says the new rates are essential to support its Delivering for America 10-year plan, which aims to stabilize USPS finances and modernize operations.

In addition to letter and postcard rates, the USPS is proposing adjustments to Special Services, including a 12% reduction in postal insurance rates — a rare cost decrease that may benefit shippers and online retailers.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Google reiterates $75B spending plan for data centers amid tariff woes

Former Treasury secretary Summers says govt 'rightly scared after collapsing markets'

EU said to weigh buying more U.S. gas, but green transition still in focus

Catalyst watch:

  • Walmart (WMT) will hold its annual shareholder meeting.

  • TD Synnex (SNX) will hold its Investor Day event.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 2.8% at $60/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.7% at $82,000. Gold is up 0.8% at $3,111.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 3.9% and the DAX is up 5.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) -4% – Shares slipped despite a Q4 beat, as the company’s FY2026 outlook disappointed investors amid trade policy uncertainty.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am CPI

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

  • 8:30 am Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will speak on"Driving Through the Economic Fog" and participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Talbot County Business Appreciation Summit sponsored by the Talbot County Chamber of Commerce.

  • 10:00 am Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid will speak on the economic outlook and monetary policy before the Secured Finance Network Independent Finance Roundtable 2025.

  • 12:00 pm Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee participates in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Economic Club of New York.

  • 12:00 pm Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Patrick Harker speaks on"Fintech" before the 2025 Fintech and Financial Institutions Research Conference.

data.title: Voya Core Plus Fixed Income SMA Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

A total return approach, investing across the full spectrum of the fixed income market, including up to 20% in below investment grade securities.

Market review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24.

The gross domestic product (GDP) report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the U.S. Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation, finished the quarter at 2.8% year over year, slightly above where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explain both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed’s Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 — down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

For the quarter, the Voya Core Plus Fixed Income SMA outperformed the Index on gross-of-fees, but slightly underperformed on net-of-fees basis. Sector allocation, duration and yield curve decisions contributed, while security selection decisions detracted. Our overweight to credit and underweight

in Treasuries broadly contributed. Our allocation to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and credit risk transfers (CRT) was a strong contributor as strong borrower characteristics continued to drive spreads tighter. Similarly, our overweight to investment grade (IG) corporates was also a meaningful contributor but was offset by negative security selection results due to our higher quality bias relative to the benchmark. Meanwhile, positive security selection results in commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) were realized due to our bias towards higher yielding tranches. Finally, due to our shorter duration profile relative to the benchmark, the move higher in rates contributed to relative performance.

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area, however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit sectors; however, we continue to favor high-quality, shorter-dated assets given where valuations are. Over the quarter, we reduced our overweight to IG corporates and are now neutral to the benchmark from a spread duration perspective. Similarly, we maintain only a modest allocation to high yield (HY) corporates, while high quality securitized makes up much of our active risk. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve. Finally, in response to the move higher in rates, we increased the strategy’s duration and are now neutral to the benchmark.

Disclaimers

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is composed of US securities in Treasury, government-related, corporate, and securitized sectors that are of investment-grade quality or better, have at least one year to maturity and have an outstanding par value of at least $250 million. Indexes do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot directly invest in an index.

Source: Bloomberg Index Services Limited. Bloomberg® is a trademark and service mark of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates (collectively “Bloomberg”). Bloomberg or Bloomberg’s licensors own all proprietary rights in the Bloomberg Indices. Bloomberg does not approve or endorse this material, nor guarantee the accuracy or completeness of any information herein, nor make any warranty, express or implied, as to the results to be obtained therefrom and, to the maximum extent allowed by law, shall not have any liability or responsibility for injury or damages arising in connection therewith.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. All security transactions involve substantial risk of loss. Please refer to your client statement for a complete review of recent transactions and performance.

The principal risks are generally those attributable to bond investing. Holdings are subject to market, issuer, credit, prepayment, extension and other risks, and their values may fluctuate. Market risk is the risk that securities may decline in value due to factors affecting the securities markets or particular industries. Issuer risk is the risk that the value of a security may decline for reasons specific to the issuer, such as changes in its financial condition. The strategy may invest in mortgage-related securities, which can be paid off early if the borrowers on the underlying mortgages pay off their mortgages sooner than scheduled. If interest rates are falling, the strategy will be forced to reinvest this money at lower yields. Conversely, if interest rates are rising, the expected principal payments will slow, thereby locking in the coupon rate at below market levels and extending the security’s life and duration while reducing its market value. High yield bonds carry particular market risks and may experience greater volatility in market value than investment grade bonds. Foreign investments could be riskier than US investments because of exchange rate, political, economic, liquidity and regulatory risks. Additionally, investments in emerging market countries are riskier than other foreign investments because the political and economic systems in emerging market countries are less stable.

The strategy employs a quantitative model to execute the strategy. Data imprecision, software or other technology malfunctions, programming inaccuracies and similar circumstances may impair the performance of these systems, which may negatively affect performance. Furthermore, there can be no assurance that the quantitative models used in managing the strategy will perform as anticipated or enable the strategy to achieve its objective.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management’s current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Fund holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors.

Disclosure for Morgan Stanley Wealth Management clients only: The content is to report on the investment strategies as reports by Voya Investment Management and is for illustrative purposes only. The information contained herein is obtained from multiple sources and believed to be reliable.
